project.name: RevCore
project.description: Audio management for Unity — clip repository, SFX pooling, music playback with volume fading. Note: this project has over 100 source files; consider scoping analysis to a subdirectory for faster results.

nodes.name: CHANGELOG.md
nodes.summary: Keep-a-changelog document tracking Audio package releases from v1.0.0 through v1.1.0, covering async fade APIs, SfxSource fixes, and UniTask integration milestones.

nodes.name: AudioAsyncExtensions.cs
nodes.summary: UniTask-based extension methods on BaseAudioManager providing async music fade and fade-out operations with cancellation token support.
nodes.languageNotes: Uses UniTaskCompletionSource for manual task lifecycle rather than coroutines, enabling cancellation-aware async patterns.

tour.title: Package Architecture Overview
tour.description: Foundation is the only zero-dependency package — every other module depends on it. The package.json manifests declare those dependency edges and set the Unity 2022.3 minimum. Reading Foundation's manifest first reveals the design contract: modular UPM packages that teams can adopt individually, with Foundation as the stable base.
tour.languageLesson: Unity UPM package.json uses 'dependencies' keys as 'com.package.name': 'version' pairs. Zero entries in Foundation means any project can import it without pulling transitive dependencies — critical for a multi-team framework.

tour.title: Foundation: Logging and Results
tour.description: RevLog and LogLevel form a replaceable logging facade: all framework code routes through RevLog so teams can swap the backend without touching framework internals. Result<T> gives callers a typed success/failure value that avoids exceptions on expected error paths, establishing the error-handling pattern used across all modules.

tour.title: Foundation: EventBus and ServiceLocator
tour.description: EventBus and ServiceLocator are the two cross-cutting services that make modules talk to each other without direct coupling. AudioManager, for example, subscribes to UISfxTriggeredEvent through EventBus rather than importing UI code directly. ServiceLocator provides type-keyed dependency lookup, letting test setups swap implementations without changing production code.
tour.languageLesson: C# generic constraints (where T : struct) on EventBus publish/subscribe enforce that event payloads are value types, eliminating heap allocations on the hot publish path — relevant for high-frequency game-loop events.

tour.title: Foundation: Diagnostics and Helpers
tour.description: RevDiagnostics defines an IRevDiagnostics listener hook that the framework calls on key operations (timer ticks, pool spawns, event publishes). The Samples directory shows three concrete listeners — crash-buffer, debug overlay, and Unity Console — illustrating how to plug in without modifying framework code. TimeHelper and TransformHelper are the utility extensions used throughout higher modules.

tour.title: Foundation: Shared Types
tour.description: BigNumber, SerializableDictionary, and PerfectRatio are shared value types that appear across Data, UI, and other modules. BigNumber handles game-economy large values with short-suffix formatting. SerializableDictionary solves Unity's inspector serialization gap for Dictionary. Understanding these types prevents confusion when they appear as fields in higher-level classes.

tour.title: Timer: Contracts and Core
tour.description: Timer depends on Foundation and UniTask. ITimerScheduler and ITimerHandle define the public surface: handles expose lifecycle state and cancellation; the scheduler covers delays, conditions, debounce, and queue. TimerScheduler implements ITimerScheduler, while CountdownTimer advances individual handles each frame — separating scheduling policy from tick mechanics.
tour.languageLesson: C# interface segregation: ITimerHandle exposes only read/cancel operations while ITimerScheduler exposes factory methods. Consumers that only need to cancel a timer depend on the smaller interface, not the full scheduler.

tour.title: Timer: Async Extension and Package
tour.description: TimersAsync provides UniTask-based overloads layered on top of ITimerScheduler, enabling await-style timer usage without blocking coroutines. The package.json shows Timer's two explicit dependencies: Foundation and UniTask — making it the first module that adds an external dependency and a useful reference for how optional async is wired in.

tour.title: Audio: Playback Stack
tour.description: BaseAudioManager is the highest-fanin file in the graph (5 dependents) because it contains all music and SFX playback logic including SFX source pooling and DOTween/coroutine volume fading. AudioManager is the thin singleton MonoBehaviour that subscribes to UISfxTriggeredEvent and delegates to BaseAudioManager. SfxSource wraps a per-object AudioSource for localised SFX with pitch randomisation and concurrency limits.

tour.title: Audio: Async Fades and Package
tour.description: AudioAsyncExtensions adds FadeMusicAsync and FadeOutMusicAsync as UniTask extension methods on BaseAudioManager, providing cancellation-token support for scene transitions. This file has fanIn 3 — tests, AudioManager, and external consumers all import it — making it a key integration point. The changelog records the v1.1.0 milestone when async fades and UniTask were added.

tour.title: Pool: RevPool and PoolObject
tour.description: RevPool is a generic component pool with a cap-eviction strategy: when the pool is full and a new object is needed, it evicts the oldest active item. PoolObject is the MonoBehaviour base that pooled prefabs inherit, giving them OnSpawn/OnRelease callbacks. PoolsContainer groups multiple RevPool instances under one MonoBehaviour for scene organisation.
tour.languageLesson: Unity object pools avoid per-spawn GC by recycling MonoBehaviours. RevPool's cap-eviction design is a fixed-budget pattern: the pool never exceeds MaxItems, which bounds memory and avoids unbounded growth during burst spawning.

tour.title: Prefs: Typed PlayerPrefs Wrappers
tour.description: Prefs wraps Unity's stringly-typed PlayerPrefs with generic, dirty-tracking wrappers (PlayerPrefBool, Int, Float, String). PlayerPrefContainer holds a static registry of all wrappers and exposes batched Save and DeleteAll operations. IPref defines the contract so tests can substitute fakes. PlayerPref (the base) has the highest fanIn in Prefs (5 dependents) because every typed subclass inherits it.

tour.title: Data: JObject Persistence Layer
tour.description: JObjectDB is a static facade managing typed JObjectData collections backed by PlayerPrefs JSON. IJObjectModel and IJObjectHandler define the contracts for data objects and their lifecycle hooks (pause, post-load, pre-save, remote config). JObjectDBManager is the MonoBehaviour orchestrator wiring auto-save timers, app-pause saves, and remote config refresh. This trio is the highest-fanOut cluster in the Data package.

tour.title: UI: Panel System and Components
tour.description: RevCore.UI provides panel navigation, a pool-backed optimised scroll view, layout helpers, safe-area support, and a tutorial mask. The README has the highest fanOut of any document node (5 edges) because it references almost every runtime component. HorizontalAlignmentUI has fanIn 2, appearing in multiple scroll and layout contexts. The UI package optionally depends on Timer and Pool when animations are needed.

tour.title: Inspector: Editor Attributes
tour.description: Inspector adds zero-runtime-overhead editor attributes: AutoFill auto-populates null serialized fields from project assets, ShowIf conditionally hides inspector fields, Highlight and ReadOnly improve editor UX. ShowIfDrawer uses cached reflection-compiled bool accessors for performance. This package has zero runtime dependencies so it can be added to any project without pulling Foundation.

tour.title: Public API Governance and Test Infrastructure
tour.description: Each package carries PublicAPI.Shipped.txt and PublicAPI.Unshipped.txt files consumed by the Roslyn PublicApiAnalyzer. Adding any public member without updating Unshipped.txt triggers a build error (RS0016), enforcing intentional API surface control. The Foundation test assembly has fanIn 6 — it is the most-depended-on test assembly — because all module test suites reference its NUnit and PerformanceTesting setup. The _Analyzers README explains the dormant-by-default activation workflow.
tour.languageLesson: Roslyn analyzer DLLs in a Unity project must carry the RoslynAnalyzer asset label to activate. Without the label the DLL compiles as a regular managed assembly — the dormant pattern lets the repo ship the analyzer without activating it on legacy assemblies that would generate too many warnings.
